vulnerability.title: The Chronyd service is enabled
vulnerability.description: chrony is a daemon which implements the Network Time Protocol (NTP) is designed to synchronize system clocks across a variety of systems and use a source that is highly accurate. More information on chrony can be found at https://chrony-project.org/. Chrony can be configured to be a client and/or a server. To enable Chronyd service, you can run: # systemctl enable chronyd.service This recommendation only applies if chrony is in use on the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q chrony; }; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'chronyd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'chronyd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'chronyd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Enabling the chronyd service is a standard, low‑impact operation that only starts and enables a time‑synchronization daemon. It does not modify authentication, bootloader, or critical system configuration and therefore poses minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: system time may be unsynchronized if chronyd fails to start; dependency on network connectivity for NTP

review_feedback: The plan creates a minimal configuration and restarts chronyd, but it does not ensure the service is enabled to start on boot, which is the core of the finding. Additionally, the generic 'allow' directive opens the NTP service to any host, which may be unnecessary and increase exposure. Adding an enable step, tightening access controls, and verifying the package is installed would make the remediation complete and more secure.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan creates a minimal configuration and restarts chronyd, but it does not ensure the service is enabled to start on boot, which is the core of the finding. Additionally, the generic 'allow' directive opens the NTP service to any host, which may be unnecessary and increase exposure. Adding an enable step, tightening access controls, and verifying the package is installed would make the remediation complete and more secure.
concerns: Missing 'systemctl enable chronyd.service' to satisfy the enabled‑service requirement.
The 'allow' line permits unrestricted NTP access, potentially widening the attack surface.
No verification that the chrony package is installed before creating the config.
No backup of any existing /etc/chrony.conf in case of custom configurations.
suggested_improvements: Add a command to enable the service permanently: systemctl enable chronyd.service.
Restrict the 'allow' directive to specific trusted subnets or remove it if not required.
Check for the presence of the chrony package and install it if missing.
Validate the configuration syntax before restarting the daemon.
